parts.answer: The only correct answer is D (6) A is incorrect because this is the number of types of ligand B is incorrect because this is the oxidation number of chromium C is incorrect because this is the number of ligands (1) number Answer Mark

The only correct answer is B (+1) A is incorrect because there is a Cr 3+ ion, two Cl − ligands and the NH CH CH NH ligands are neutral C is incorrect because there is a Cr 3+ ion, two Cl − ligands and the NH CH CH NH ligands are neutral D is incorrect because there is a Cr 3+ ion, two Cl − ligands and the NH CH CH NH ligands are neutral (1) number Answer Mark The only correct answer is A ([Al(H O) ] 3+ ) B is incorrect because [Cu(H O) ] 2+ gives a blue solution C is incorrect because [Fe(H O) ] 3+ gives an orange / brown solution D is incorrect because [Ni(H O) ] 2+ gives a green solution (1) number Answer Mark The only correct answer is D (Zn(OH) ) A is incorrect because Cu(OH) is only soluble in excess aqueous ammonia B is incorrect because Fe(OH) is insoluble in both excess aqueous ammonia and excess aqueous sodium hydroxide C is incorrect because Ni(OH) is only soluble in excess aqueous ammonia (1) number Answer Mark The only correct answer is C (24.4 %) A is incorrect because 6.10 % is the value when only 1 mol of water is considered B is incorrect because 8.06 % is the value when only 1 mol of water is considered and no water has been included in the molar mass of the salt D is incorrect because 32.2 % is the value when no water has been included in the molar mass of the salt (1) number Answer Mark The only correct answer is C (diagram C with peak at (0.001, 8)) A is incorrect because the complex ion with EDTA 4− has a more intense colour intensity than that with CN − ions B is incorrect because EDTA 4− is a hexadentate ligand so the mol ratio Cr 3+; EDTA 4− is 1 : 1 and the colour intensity should be higher D is incorrect because EDTA 4− is a hexadentate ligand so the mol ratio Cr 3+; EDTA 4− is 1 : 1 (1) number Answer Mark The only correct answer is D (activation energy is high) A is incorrect because a positive value for E o cell indicates the reaction is thermodynamically feasible B is incorrect because a positive value for ∆ r H would not be affected by

The only correct answer is B (3095 – 3010 cm −1 ) A is incorrect because 3500 – 3300 cm −1 shows the presence of N-H in an amine which is present in valine C is incorrect because 2962 – 2853 cm −1 shows the presence of C-H in an alkane which is present in valine D is incorrect because 1725 – 1720 cm −1 shows the presence of C=O in an carboxylic acid which is present in valine (1) number Answer Mark

The only correct answer is A (doublet and octet) B is incorrect because the peak corresponding to the H attached to C with 2 CH groups and CH will be an octet as there are 7 protons on the neighbouring carbons C is incorrect because the peak corresponding to the 2 CH groups will be a doublet as there is 1 proton on the neighbouring carbon D is incorrect because the peak corresponding to the 2 CH groups will be a doublet as there is 1 proton on the neighbouring carbon and the H attached to C with 2 CH groups and CH will be an octet as there are 7 protons on the neighbouring carbons (1) number Answer Mark

The only correct answer is D (nucleophilic addition) A is incorrect because electrophiles attack electron rich regions but the carbon atom attached to the magnesium is δ− B is incorrect because the Grignard reagent is not a source of free radicals C is incorrect because increasing the length of the carbon chain is not oxidation (1) number Answer Mark

The only correct answer is B ( CH CH CH(CH )MgBr) A is incorrect because this would form 1-phenylpentan-1-ol C is incorrect because this would form 1-phenyl-3-methylbutan-1-ol D is incorrect because this would form 1-phenyl-2,2-dimethylpropan-1-ol (1) number Answer Mark The only correct answer is C (0.6 ) A is incorrect because the amino acid in the lower spot will have an R f value of about 0.2 B is incorrect because the distance moved by X is measured from the solvent front instead of from the baseline D is incorrect because the amino acid in the higher spot will have an R f value of about 0.8 (1) number Mark The only correct answer is D ( and ) A is incorrect because amines do not react with carboxylic acids B is incorrect because amides do react to form polyamides C is incorrect because this pair of monomers will not produce the required polyamide (1) number Answer Mark

The only correct answer is A (NaNO and HCl at 5 °C) B is incorrect because NaNO does not react with HCl to form the nitrous acid needed for the formation of benzenediazonium ions C is incorrect because nitrous acid and benzenediazonium ions decompose at 50°C D is incorrect because NaNO does not react with HCl to form the nitrous acid needed for the formation of benzenediazonium ions and nitrous acid and benzenediazonium ions decompose at 50°C (1) number Answer Mark

The only correct answer is B ( in alkaline solution) A is incorrect because alkaline conditions are needed to form an azo dye C is incorrect because the OH group is in the wrong position and alkaline conditions are needed to form an azo dye D is incorrect because the OH group is in the wrong position (1) number Answer Mark The only correct answer is A ( ) B is incorrect because the OH group will not be protonated in preference to the NH group C is incorrect because the addition of an acid will cause protonation not loss of a proton D is incorrect because the addition of an acid will cause protonation not loss of protons (1) (Total for Section A = 20 marks) Section B Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark

 calculation of mol C / CO (1)  calculation of mol H (1)  calculation of mol of O (1)  formula (1) Comment – alternative method via moles of A  M1 calculate moles of A : 5.26÷136 =0.0387 (mol)  M2 calculate moles of CO and H O  M3 use combustion equation to show A forms 9CO and 6H O, so C=9 and H=12  M4 use of Mr to show mass due to O = 16, so number of O atoms = 1 If candidate does not score 4 marks, mark using method that gives best score Example of calculation: mol CO = 15.3/44 = 0.34773 = mol C or mass C = 15.3 x 12/44 = 4.1727 (g) and mol C = 4.1727/12 = 0.34773 mol H O = 4.18/18 = 0.23222 and mol H = 2 x 0.23222 = 0.46444 or mass H = 4.18 x 2/18 = 0.46444 (g) and mol H = 0.46444 mass O = 5.26 – 4.1727 – 0.46444 = 0.62286 (g) and mol O = 0.62286/16 = 0.038929 or moles of O = 5.26/136 = 0.03876 (mol) TE on mass C and H ratio mol 0.34773 C : 0.46444 H : 0.038929 O 0.038929 0.038929 0.038929 9 12 1 So formula is C H O (and this is the same as the molecular formula as M r = (9 x 12) + (12 x 1) + 16 = 136) No TE on incorrect mol Ignore SF except 1 SF at each stage Comment : Ignore minor rounding errors e.g. 4.172 is acceptable for mass of C Allow masses of C, H and O to be determined and expressed as percentages e.g. C = 79.4%, H = 8.8%, so O = 11.8% Allow alternative methods (4) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark

 structure of A (1)  structure of B (1)  structure of C (1)  structure of D (1)  structure of E (1) Examples of structures Ignore connectivity of OH CHI (standalone mark) Allow E or Z isomer or both isomers Allow displayed / structural formulae or any combination of these / skeletal formulae Allow Kekule Structures Allow D TE on incorrect 2° alcohol A e.g. if A is C H CH(OH)C H Allow E as TE on incorrect 1° alcohol A e.g. if A is C H CH CH CH OH If D and E are the wrong way around, allow (1) for M4 and M5 (5) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark

An explanation that makes reference to the following points:  structure of F (1)  carbon atoms labelled (1) Example of structure: Allow displayed / structural formulae or any combination of these / skeletal formulae Allow alternative clear ways of identifying carbon atoms Allow M2 dependent on M1, unless very near miss (e.g. accidental omitting H on OH group) (2) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark

An answer that makes reference to the following points:  structure of G (1)  m / z corresponds to C H O + / C H CHOH + / C H OCH + / C H CH O + / loss of C H (1) Example of structure OR OR Allow any combination of structural and displayed formula / skeletal formula – if 2 structures are shown both must be incorrect Ignore missing + M1 and M2 are standalone marks No TE from incorrect structure (2) (Total for Question 17 = 13 marks) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark
 +5 / 5+ Allow V / 5 / V 5+ (1) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark

An explanation that makes reference to the following points:  V 5+ / the vanadium ion is (very) small / highly charged (1)  so it would polarise (two) water molecules / OH bonds (1)  causing them to lose hydrogen ions / H + ions / deprotonate (1) Allow high charge density Allow ‘so it weakens OH bonds’ Allow ‘distorts electron clouds in water’ Allow the energy required to remove 5 electrons to form V 5+ is too high (1) because the energy is not recovered by the hydration of the ion (1) If no marks given allow 1 mark for correct electronic configuration of V 5+ e.g. [Ar] (3) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark

 calculation of mol of each element (1)  deduction of mol ratio (and empirical formula) (1)  overall formula (1) Example of calculation: K V S H O moles 7.9 39.1 = 0.202 10.2 50.9 = 0.200 12.9 32.1 = 0.402 4.8 = 4.8 64.2 = 4.01 divide by smallest 0.202 0.200 = 1 0.200 0.200 = 1 0.402 0.200 = 2 4.8 0.200 = 24 4.01 0.200 = 20 Empirical formula KVS H O Ignore minor rounding errors in M1 and M2 TE on mol ratio from M1 Example of overall formula: KV(SO ) •12H O or K SO •V (SO ) •24H O Allow KV(SO ) (H O) Allow K SO •V (SO ) (H O) Ignore SF Allow the ions in any order / correct charges shown by individual ions, even if charges are not shown on all ions / missing dot(s) No TE from M2 to M3 (3) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  calculation of mol of V 3+ (1)  calculation of mol MnO − (1)  calculation of ratio of MnO − to V 3+ (1) Example of calculation: Mol V 3+ = 10.0 x 0.132 = 0.00132 / 1.32 x 10 −3 (mol) Mol MnO − = 26.40 x 0.0200 = 0.000528 / 5.28 x 10 −4 (mol) Ratio MnO − : V 3+ = 0.000528 : 0.00132 = 1 : 2.5 or 2 : 5 Allow TE on M1 and M2 Ignore SF except 1 SF in calculation of moles Allow 5 to 2 if it’s clear it’s V 3+ : MnO − (3) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  correct species on each side of equation (1)  balancing (1) Comment – M2 dependent on M1 Example of equation: 2MnO − + 5V 3+ + 2H O → 2Mn 2+ + 5VO + + 4H + Or 2MnO − + 5V 3+ + 22H O → 2Mn 2+ + 5[VO (H O) ] + + 4H + Allow multiples Allow (1) for 2MnO − + 5V 3+ + 16H + → 2Mn 2+ + 5V 5+ + 8H O Ignore state symbols even if incorrect Allow oxidation to V(IV) if ratio 1 : 5 in (d)(i) MnO − + 5V 3+ + H O → Mn 2+ + 5VO 2+ + 2H + species (1) balancing (1) (2) Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark logically structured answer with linkages and fully-sustained reasoning. Marks are awarded for indicative content and for how the answer is structured and shows lines of reasoning. The following table shows how the marks should be awarded for indicative content. Number of indicative marking

An answer that makes reference to the following points:  Reagent for step 1: potassium cyanide and (aqueous) ethanol (1)  First intermediate: 2-phenylethanenitrile (1)  Reagent for step 2: lithium tetrahydridoaluminate(III) / LiAlH in (dry) ether (followed by (hydrolysis with) dilute acid / H + ) (1)  Second intermediate: 2-phenylethylamine (1)  Reagent for step 3: ethanoyl chloride / CH COCl / ethanoic anhydride / (CH CO) O (1) Allow displayed / structural formulae or any combination of these / skeletal formulae for intermediates Ignore any references to heat / reflux throughout Ignore HCN Allow NaCN Stand alone mark Allow -CN (i.e.triple bond not displayed) Do not award H and Ni / Pt / Pd Stand alone mark Allow TE from M2, if extra C shown in nitrile Do not award ethanoic acid / CH COOH Ignore AlCl (5) Comment – allow (3) for use of Grignard reagent; M1 –formation of Grignard, then reaction with HCHO to form C H CH CH OH, then reaction with KBr / H SO to form C H CH CH Br, then reaction with NH M2 structure of C H CH CH NH M3 reaction of C H CH CH NH with CH COCl Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  structure of zwitterion Example of zwitterion: Allow ‘+’ anywhere on NH group Allow carboxylate ion shown with charge delocalised across two oxygen atoms Allow displayed / structural / skeletal formulae or any combination of these Ignore bond lengths and bond angles (1) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  structure of dipeptide Example of dipeptide: Allow displayed / structural / skeletal formulae or any combination of these Allow C H for the phenyl groups Ignore bond lengths and bond angles Ignore connectivity of OH unless displayed as C-H-O (i.e. a bond shown from C to H then to O) (1) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  C H N O Allow symbols in any order e.g. C H O N (1) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  two chiral carbon atoms circled Example of circled chiral carbons: Allow other ways of representing the two carbon atoms e.g. asterisk * I

 correct balanced equation Example of equation: 3MnO 2− + 4H + → 2MnO − + MnO + 2H O Allow multiples Allow reversible arrows provided reactants as shown are still on LHS Allow uncancelled electrons on either side Ignore state symbols even if incorrect Ignore oxidation states above atoms, even if incorrect (1) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark

An explanation that makes reference to the following points:  this reaction is (auto)catalysed by the Mn 2+ ions formed (1)  (the reaction in) experiment 1 starts slowly because there is no Mn 2+ / catalyst present initially (but speeds up as Mn 2+ ions are formed) (1)  (the reaction in) experiment 2 is fast(est) at the start as Mn 2+ ions / catalyst (already) present (1) Mn 2+ can be mentioned at any point Allow experiment 1 starts slowly but speeds up as Mn 2+ / catalyst forms Allow rate decreases constantly as Mn 2+ ions / catalyst (already) present (3) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  calculation of mol of KMnO (1)  calculation of mol of C H CH (1)  KMnO is in excess because there are more than twice as many mol of KMnO than mol of C H CH (1) Example of calculation: Mol KMnO = 7.00 = 0.044304 (mol) Mol C H CH = 1.73 = 0.018804 (mol) Accept 0.044304 mol KMnO would react with 0.022152 mol C H CH or reverse argument TE on M1 and M2 Allow other methods e.g. Mol C H CH = 1.73 = 0.018804 (mol) (1) Minimum mass of KMnO needed = 2 x 0.018804 x 158 = 5.9421 (g) (1) This is less than 7 g so KMnO is in excess (1) Ignore SF except 1 SF in M1 and M2 (3) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  add H + ions / acidify the solution / mixture Allow correct name or formula of any strong acid e.g. HCl, H SO , HNO , H PO Do not award carboxylic acids e.g. CH COOH Allow C H CO – + H + → C H COOH Ignore references to concentration / heat / reflux Do not award ‘acid hydrolysis’ / acid catalyst / H + ions from water / inclusion of a second incorrect reagent e.g. H + and LiAlH (1) Number Answer Additional Guidance Mark  anode half-equation (1)  cathode half-equation (1) Examples of equations: Zn + 2OH − → ZnO + H O + 2e − 2MnO + H O + 2e − → Mn O + 2OH − Allow multiples / reversible arrows Ignore state symbols even if incorrect If no other mark is awarded allow (1) for anode and cathode half-equations written in wrong places If no other mark awarded allow 1 mark for Zn on
